narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real promise in several areas, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—which is above the typical student's 3.21—and a strong LSAT score of 38, higher than the average of 36.70. Your family comes from an upper-middle income background, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72.5% probability that you would fail it. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this prediction to ensure they admit students likely to succeed on the bar. The biggest factors pushing toward this failure prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is much lower than the average of about 5.5 and signaled weaker performance relative to peers. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also well below the typical 5.7, was the next strongest influence in the same concerning direction. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA and LSAT scores each pulled strongly in the opposite way, toward a prediction of passing the bar. Your full-time status and family income had smaller positive influences too, aligning with what's common for successful students. Compared to typical applicants, your early academic deciles stand out as unusually low, overshadowing your other strengths and differing from the mid-range deciles most bar passers have. While this decision stings, especially given the barriers Black women like you often face in competitive fields, it's not the end of your journey. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your school performance to climb those deciles—perhaps through tutoring, study groups, or time management strategies. You might also retake the LSAT for an even higher score or seek programs offering bar prep support tailored to diverse students. Keep pushing forward; your determination can rewrite this story.
